ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic pilaf entry (FOODON_00002678); no Xinjiang-specific or Uyghur pilaf entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a Xinjiang pilaf-specific code. A0FKP (rice dish with meat) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Xinjiang pilaf or Uyghur polo. SNOMED CT does not cover regional rice dish variants at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Xinjiang pilaf is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous mound of rice with lamb pieces, carrots, and onions, as typically served at a Chinese restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Pilaf, lamb and rice (composite estimate from USDA Foundation Foods lamb shoulder and long-grain white rice, with carrot and onion components)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Xinjiang pilaf exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked long-grain white rice (FDC ID 169756), braised lamb shoulder (FDC ID 174352), cooked carrots (FDC ID 170393), and cooked onions (FDC ID 170000), combined in approximate proportions of 55% rice, 30% lamb, 10% carrot, 5% onion by weight, with added oil (~8 g per 100 g dish). Values represent a plausible per-100 g composite for a restaurant-style Xinjiang pilaf.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice pilaf / mixed rice dish with meat
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Xinjiang pilaf. GI estimated at 54 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice pilaf with meat and vegetables (GI range 50–56 for mixed rice-meat dishes with fat and protein moderating glycemic response). The fat and protein content of lamb and oil in this dish lower the effective GI relative to plain white rice (GI ~72). GI of 54 is consistent with published values for comparable pilaf-style dishes. gi_category set to 'low' per threshold rule (GI ≤ 55 = low). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 88.0) / 100 = 47.5, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 22.0 × 4 = 88.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 280 mg per 100 g; a 400 g serving contributes approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request reduced salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute a substantial proportion of the daily sodium budget.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (estimated GI 54) and high carbohydrate load per serving (approximately 88 g carbohydrate per 400 g plate); glycemic load per serving is high at approximately 47.5.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume a reduced portion (150–200 g) of Xinjiang pilaf, pair it with non-starchy vegetables, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the high glycemic load of a full serving may cause significant blood glucose elevation despite the moderate GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lamb (approximately 2.8 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium content; a full serving provides approximately 11.2 g saturated fat, approaching the daily limit for individuals with cardiovascular disease.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Xinjiang pilaf to a half-portion (approximately 200 g), choose leaner lamb cuts where possible, and balance the meal with high-fibre vegetables to mitigate the saturated fat and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Xinjiang Pilaf (新疆手抓饭, Xīnjiāng shǒuzhuā fàn) is a traditional one-pot rice dish originating from the Xinjiang Uyghur Autonomous Region of northwestern China, where it is known as 'polo' in the Uyghur language. The dish is a Central Asian-influenced preparation in which long-grain rice is cooked together with braised or stewed lamb, julienned carrots, sliced onions, and generous amounts of vegetable or cottonseed oil, seasoned with cumin, salt, and sometimes dried raisins or garlic. The name 'shǒuzhuā fàn' (手抓饭) literally means 'hand-grabbed rice', reflecting the traditional practice of eating the dish by hand. The rice absorbs the lamb fat and aromatic cooking juices, resulting in a rich, savoury, slightly oily texture with tender meat and sweet caramelised carrots. In Singapore, Xinjiang pilaf is served at specialist northern Chinese and Uyghur-style restaurants such as Bei Jiang Restaurant, typically as a main course priced around SGD 19. Nutritionally, a 400 g restaurant serving provides approximately 740 kcal, 33 g protein, 30 g fat, and 88 g carbohydrate. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal. The moderate glycemic index (estimated GI 54) is moderated by the fat and protein content of the lamb. Clinically, the high glycemic load per serving and moderate sodium content make portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Xinjiang lamb rice?
What does hand-grabbed rice taste like?
Where can I eat Xinjiang pilaf in Singapore?
Is Xinjiang pilaf good for diabetics?
What are the main ingredients in Xinjiang pilaf?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Xinjiang pilaf was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked white rice, braised lamb shoulder, cooked carrots, and cooked onions in approximate proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable pilaf-style mixed rice-meat dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component data (cooked long-grain white rice FDC 169756, braised lamb shoulder FDC 174352, cooked carrots FDC 170393, cooked onions FDC 170000) in approximate proportions 55% rice, 30% lamb, 10% carrot, 5% onion, with ~8 g added oil per 100 g dish. These are plausible estimates; exact restaurant recipe proportions at Bei Jiang Restaurant are not publicly available. 2. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 54) based on University of Sydney GI Database values for white rice pilaf with meat and vegetables; no direct GI measurement for Xinjiang pilaf was found in the approved GI literature. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in the USDA component sources used; lamb and rice contribute negligible vitamin D and the value is not reported in the source database entries used for estimation. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g per 100 g reflects naturally occurring trans fats from ruminant lamb fat (conjugated linoleic acid and vaccenic acid), not industrial trans fats. 5. The cuisine_tag 'Noodle' in the input record appears to be a field survey platform tagging error; Xinjiang pilaf is a rice dish, not a noodle dish. This tag has not been carried forward. 6. Serving size of 400 g is estimated based on typical restaurant portion sizes for rice-based main courses in Singapore. 7. Halal status is assessed as likely true given the Uyghur Muslim culinary tradition and the restaurant context, but formal halal certification for Bei Jiang Restaurant Boat Quay has not been independently verified from an approved source.
